**Problem.** `.distinctOn(...)` (builders.ts) and `.for(...)` row locking render
**unconditionally** — there is no dialect guard. Calling them on a SQLite-family
database produces SQL the engine rejects, so misuse surfaces as a raw database
**syntax error** rather than a typed `OrmError` that names the real cause. The
audit confirmed this against live libSQL.

**Proposed work.** When a Postgres-only construct (`distinctOn`, `.for(...)`
locking, the array operators) is rendered against a non-Postgres dialect, throw
a clear `OrmError` (e.g.
`"distinctOn is PostgreSQL-only; the sqlite dialect has no
equivalent"`) at
render time, before execution. This is the "parity where it is _not_ possible"
half of the mission: the limit stays, but it becomes graceful and
self-explaining.

**Affected.** `@sisal/orm` (`builders.ts` render path, `errors.ts`), parity
tests in `packages/{sqlite}/drizzle_parity_test.ts`.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- Rendering `distinctOn` / `.for(...)` / array operators on a SQLite-family
  dialect throws a typed `OrmError` naming the construct and the dialect.
- Postgres/Neon behavior is unchanged.
- A unit test pins the thrown error for each construct per dialect.

---

## 5. Document principled divergences & round-trip behavior — P2

**Problem.** The genuine, permanent differences (Tier 1 limits and Tier 2
round-trip shapes from the audit) are described unevenly across the four docs.
The unified matrix (item 3) needs one canonical explanation to link its
`❌`/`⚠️` cells to.

**Proposed work.** A single "Driver capability & round-trip reference" section
that states, once: which constructs are Postgres-only and _why_ the
SQLite-family cannot offer them (no `ILIKE` keyword, no array type, no stored
functions, no row-level locking), and the canonical round-trip table
(`json`/array → `TEXT`, `boolean` → `0/1`, `bytea` → `ArrayBuffer` on libSQL,
`numeric`/`bigint` → number vs precision-string). The per-engine behavior notes
then reference it.

**Affected.** `docs/feature-matrix.md` (or a shared notes doc), the four
per-engine compatibility docs, [drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md) footnote ⁸
(already marks the array operators Postgres-only).

**Acceptance criteria.**

- Every `❌` and `⚠️` cell in the unified matrix links to a one-paragraph
  reason.
- No conflicting or duplicated explanations remain across the per-engine docs.

---

## 6. Coverage-honesty: CHANGELOG fix + CI parity guard — P3

**Problem.** Two honesty issues the audit found. (a) The `Unreleased` `db.batch`
CHANGELOG entry says it is _"Covered by … the gated integration suites"_, but
the **Neon** suite has no batch test — the claim overstates current coverage.
(b) Nothing stops the matrices from drifting from the suites again.

**Proposed work.** Correct the `db.batch` changelog wording to name the suites
that actually cover it (pg/sqlite/libsql) until item 2 lands, then update it.
Add a lightweight check (extending `tools/` or the integration workflow) that
parses the unified matrix's `✅` cells and asserts a correspondingly-named test
exists in the matching suite — failing CI when a matrix claims coverage that no
test backs (mirroring how the drizzle-parity tests pin that doc).

**Affected.** `CHANGELOG.md`, `tools/` (new parity-coverage check),
`.github/workflows` integration job, the unified matrix's source of truth.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- The `db.batch` changelog line matches reality.
- A CI check fails if the unified matrix marks a feature ✅ for an adapter whose
  suite has no corresponding test.
- The check is documented next to `docs:check` in CLAUDE.md's commands list.

---

## 7. Richer DDL: functions & triggers — P2 (deferred from v0.4.0 item 5)

**Context.** [v0.4.0 item 5](v0.4.0-roadmap.md) shipped richer **index** DDL
(per-column `ASC`/`DESC`, partial `WHERE`, and `` sql`...` `` expression keys)
and already emitted `CHECK` constraints inline. The remaining sub-item — 5.3,
explicitly the "stretch" — is **functions and triggers**, which is a larger
design than an index tweak and so moves here.

**Problem.** The DDL generators emit only schema objects derived from
`defineTable` (tables, columns, constraints, indexes). They cannot express a
stored `CREATE FUNCTION` / `CREATE TRIGGER` body, so an app like
`examples/neon-hot-feed` keeps its `app.vote_post` function and hot-score
function as hand-written `.sql` migrations and treats `src/schema.ts` as a typed
mirror rather than the generator's source of truth.

**Proposed work.** A way to attach raw, dialect-specific DDL fragments
(functions, triggers, extensions) to a schema snapshot so generated migrations
include them verbatim, ordered **after** table/column/constraint creation. This
needs design for: ordering relative to tables, `down`/drop generation,
drift/checksum handling for opaque SQL bodies, and per-dialect gating (Postgres
functions vs. SQLite triggers differ).

**Affected.** `@sisal/orm` (`schema.ts` snapshot — a new
`functions`/`schemaObjects` array; `table.ts` or a new `defineSchemaObject`
helper), each adapter DDL generator, and `@sisal/migrate` diff classification.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- A snapshot can carry ordered raw DDL fragments emitted after table creation,
  with matching drop statements for `down`.
- `examples/neon-hot-feed` can regenerate its full `0001_init.sql` (functions
  included) from `src/schema.ts`.
- Drift detection accounts for changed function/trigger bodies.
- Move the corresponding [drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md) rows.

**Status (🟡 in progress).** The core landed: `createSchemaSnapshot` accepts a
`schemaObjects` array of raw, dialect-gated DDL fragments
(`SisalSchemaObjectSnapshot` — function/trigger/extension/view/raw, with `up`,
optional `down`, optional `dialect`). The three additive DDL generators emit the
selected objects **after** their index loop, in declared order; an optional
`dialect` gates each object to one engine (a Postgres-only function is skipped
by the SQLite-family generator), and dialect-agnostic objects emit everywhere.
New `@sisal/orm` exports: `defineSchemaObject`, `selectSchemaObjects` (the
dialect+change filter the generators call), and `schemaObjectDropStatements`
(reverse-order `down`). Verified by `packages/orm/schema_test.ts`, pg/sqlite
render tests, and a `schema objects` integration test in all four suites (now 35
each) running a real trigger + view on PostgreSQL 18, Neon, SQLite, and libSQL.
Down emission and drift now land too:
`schemaObjectDropStatements(snapshot,
dialect)` gives the matching reverse-order
drops (a pg integration test applies the up then the down and asserts the
function/view are gone), and because `schemaObjects` are part of the normalized
snapshot, `equalSchemaSnapshots` — and therefore `checkDrift` — already flag a
changed function/trigger **body** (pinned by a `@sisal/migrate` test).
Regenerating `examples/neon-hot-feed` surfaced one more gap — the builder's
`.default()` took only a literal or a JS client-side function, so a column
couldn't express a **SQL-expression server default** (`gen_random_uuid()`,
`now()`), even though the snapshot (`SisalColumnDefault.kind === "expression"`)
and the generators already emit one. That landed as a
`.default(sql\`…\`)`overload (stored as`ColumnDefinition.sqlDefault`; the column is omitted on
insert so the database fills it — no insert-builder change needed). With it,`neon-hot-feed`now **generates** its full init (tables, DESC indexes, the
CHECK, and both functions moved into`schemaObjects`) from`src/schema.ts`— no
hand-written`.sql`files — verified end-to-end against real Neon. The
[drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md) matrix had no schema-object row to move
(Drizzle has no structured function/trigger concept), so Sisal's`schemaObjects`
is ahead rather than at parity. **Item 7 is complete.**

---

## 8. Portable atomic "transaction script" — P2 (procedure parity)

**Context.** The
[`neon-rising-feed`](../examples/postgres-family-feed/README.md) ↔
[`libsql-rising-feed`](../examples/sqlite-family-feed/README.md) pair builds the
identical `/rising` feed on both engines. The activity recorder — dedupe the
actor, bump a counter, bump `unique_actors` only on the actor's first touch,
recompute the bucket score — is the same logic, but it reads **completely
differently** per engine because SQLite has no stored procedures:

- **Neon:** one `app.record_post_activity(...)` function, called as a single
  atomic statement through the typed `db.call` surface — Deno-Deploy friendly.
- **libSQL:** the same steps reimplemented in TypeScript inside an interactive
  `db.transaction(...)` (`insert().onConflictDoNothing().returning()` to detect
  a new actor, an `onConflictDoUpdate` upsert with raw-`sql` increments, a
  `select()` readback).

Both are clean, but the application code is duplicated and shaped by the engine,
not the domain.

**Problem.** There is no Sisal-level way to express "run these steps atomically"
that compiles to a **database function call where one exists** and an
**interactive transaction otherwise**. `db.batch` is close but forbids a
statement depending on a previous one's result, which this read-modify-write
needs.

**Proposed work (design).** A "transaction script" / atomic-operation helper:
author the steps once against the builder; on Postgres-family adapters it can be
backed by (or generated into) a `CREATE FUNCTION` and invoked as one statement,
while SQLite-family adapters run it as an interactive transaction. Needs design
for: how dependent results flow between steps, how it interacts with item 7
(function DDL generation), and the serverless single-round-trip guarantee.

**Affected.** `@sisal/orm` (new API + render/execute paths), every adapter, and
item 7's DDL generation. **Acceptance:** the rising-feed recorder reads
identically in both examples; it is one round trip on Neon HTTP and one
interactive transaction on libSQL; covered on every adapter.

**Status (🟢 done).** Both paths landed. `defineAtomicOperation` (`@sisal/orm`)
authors dependent read-modify-write steps once. Called with a plain body it runs
as one **interactive transaction on every adapter**; called with a config form
`{ body, singleStatement }` it **dispatches on dialect** — PostgreSQL-family
adapters run the `singleStatement` form as one statement (no `BEGIN`/`COMMIT`
round trips — a data-modifying CTE built with item 12's mutating `WITH`, e.g.
`with u as (update … returning n) select n from u`), while the SQLite family
runs the interactive `body` in a transaction. So the serverless
**single-round-trip guarantee** is met on Neon while libSQL keeps the
interactive path, from one `op.run(db, input)` call site. Covered by
`packages/orm/atomic_test.ts` (network-free wrapping + dialect dispatch) + an
`atomic operation` and an `atomic op single-round-trip dispatch` integration
test in all four suites (asserting both engines return the identical
read-modify-write result), and a unified-matrix row. The single-statement form
is exercised end-to-end on **real Neon** by the `neon-rising-feed-ctes`
recompute (item 12c), which sends its `UPDATE … FROM` over chained CTEs in one
round trip. **By design, not a gap:** full recorder unification across the
sibling examples was deliberately not pursued — `neon-rising-feed` (stored
function) and `libsql-rising-feed` (interactive TS) keep their idiomatic
per-engine forms; `defineAtomicOperation` is the portable primitive that makes a
unified recorder _possible_ without flattening those lessons. The
generated-`CREATE FUNCTION` backing (item 7) remains a future option beyond the
CTE form.

---

## 9. `FILTER` aggregates + interval/date math + `dateTrunc` — P2

**Problem.** The moving-window score is a conditional aggregate over time
windows:

```sql
sum(activity_score) FILTER (WHERE bucket_start >= p_now - interval '15 minutes')
```

Sisal's builder has `sum`/`count`/`avg`/… but **no `FILTER (WHERE …)` clause**,
**no interval/date arithmetic**, and **no `dateTrunc`/time-bucket helper**. So
in `neon-rising-feed` the whole score lives in a raw SQL function, and in
`libsql-rising-feed` it is computed in TypeScript after reading the buckets —
and the 5-minute bucket flooring is hand-written per dialect (`app.bucket_5m` in
SQL, `bucket5mIso` in TS).

**Proposed work.** (a) A `filter(condition)` modifier on aggregate expressions
that renders `agg(...) FILTER (WHERE ...)` (Postgres) and degrades to
`agg(CASE WHEN ... THEN ... END)` on SQLite. (b) An interval/`now`-relative date
helper for `where`/expressions. (c) A portable `dateTrunc('5 minutes', col)` /
bucket helper that renders per dialect (`date_trunc` /
`to_timestamp(floor(...))` on PG, `unixepoch`/`strftime` math on SQLite).

**Affected.** `@sisal/orm` (`operators.ts`, `sql.ts`, render paths), adapter
dialect rendering. **Acceptance:** the rising-score window query is
builder-native on both engines; render tests pin the per-dialect SQL; the
examples drop their raw-SQL/TS-only window math.

**Status (🟢 done).** All three parts (a/b/c) landed as builder helpers.
`filter(agg, condition)` appends a `FILTER (WHERE …)` clause — and because
modern SQLite/libSQL support `FILTER` natively (the planned `CASE WHEN`
degradation proved unnecessary), it renders identically on every adapter. The
date math: `dateTrunc(field, source)` (calendar-field truncation), `now()`,
`dateAdd`/`dateSub(source, duration)` (interval arithmetic —
`gte(col, dateSub(now(), { minutes: 15 }))`), and `dateBin(every, source)`
(floor to an arbitrary-width bucket, e.g. 5 minutes). Each renders its own
per-dialect SQL
(`date_trunc`/`now()`/`± interval`/`to_timestamp(floor(epoch/N)*N)` on Postgres;
`strftime`/`datetime('now')`/chained `datetime` modifiers/
`datetime((unixepoch/N)*N)` on the SQLite family), built on a new public
`dialectSql` primitive (throws `ORM_DIALECT_UNSUPPORTED` when no
variant/fallback matches). New `@sisal/orm` exports: `filter`, `dateTrunc`,
`DateTruncField`, `now`, `dateAdd`, `dateSub`, `dateBin`, `DateDuration`,
`dialectSql`. Verified by `packages/orm/aggregates_test.ts` and
`filter aggregate + dateTrunc` / `date math window` integration tests on all
four engines — the latter runs the **actual rising-score moving window**
(`filter` + `dateSub` + `now`) and `dateBin` bucketing — so "the window query is
builder-native on both engines" is met (three matrix rows added). The
`neon-rising-feed-ctes` example now uses the helpers in **both** directions: the
read-only `selectRisingScore` (`src/queries.ts`) and the **recompute write**
itself (`src/rising.ts`, with item 12's `update().from()`) compute the
moving-window aggregate with `filter` + `dateSub` + `now`, verified against real
Neon to equal the prior raw-SQL CTE and the TypeScript model — so the example's
window math left the raw-SQL escape hatch entirely (write included). **By
design, not a gap:** the sibling `libsql-rising-feed` (no-stored-proc
TypeScript) and `neon-rising-feed` (stored function) keep their idiomatic
per-engine teaching forms; the helpers make the builder-native aggregate
available without flattening those distinct lessons.

---

## 10. `.optional()` should not widen the SELECT row type — P3

**Problem.** `.optional()` is documented as an **insert-only** axis that "does
not change column nullability." But marking a nullable column `.optional()` adds
`undefined` to its inferred **SELECT** type (`Date | null | undefined`), so a
plain `InferSelect` row no longer matches a hand-written `T | null` interface.
Surfaced in `neon-rising-feed`: `rising_score_updated_at` had to stay
required-on-insert (passing an explicit `null`) to keep the feed row type clean.

**Proposed work.** Make `.optional()` affect only `InferInsert` (the insert
optionality), leaving `InferSelect` as `T | null`. **Affected.** `@sisal/orm`
column/type inference. **Acceptance:** an `.optional()` nullable column infers
`T | null` on select and `T | null | undefined`-omittable on insert; a type test
pins both; the example can use `.optional()` without widening the feed row.

---

## 11. Coerce numeric reads to `number` (float8 returns as string) — P2

**Context.** Surfaced while validating the
[`neon-rising-feed`](../examples/postgres-family-feed/README.md) example against
real databases (June 2026). **`@sisal/pg` is the only adapter that returns
`double precision` as a string** — a driver-parity gap, which is exactly this
roadmap's theme. Verified first-hand, all reading the same
`306.0::double
precision`:

| Adapter         | `double precision` read | Notes                                        |
| --------------- | ----------------------- | -------------------------------------------- |
| `@sisal/pg`     | `"306"` (**string**)    | `jsr:@db/postgres` decodes `float8` → string |
| `@sisal/neon`   | `306` (number)          | `@neon/serverless` decodes `float8` → number |
| `@sisal/sqlite` | `306` (number)          | SQLite `REAL` → number                       |
| `@sisal/libsql` | `306` (number)          | SQLite `REAL` → number                       |

Integers (`int2`/`int4`) come back as numbers on `@sisal/pg`, and `timestamptz`
maps to `Temporal`/`Date`, so only the float types are inconsistent.

**Problem.** A column declared `columns.doublePrecision()` infers `number`, but
on `@sisal/pg` the runtime value is a `string`. This is a silent type lie with
real consequences:

- client-side numeric work breaks — `score.toFixed(2)` throws (`"306".toFixed`
  is not a function), and `Array.sort`/comparisons run lexicographically
  (`"180" < "72"` is `true`);
- a keyset/ORDER BY whose tiebreaker is a float column still _orders correctly
  in the database_ (the DB casts the bound string param), so the bug hides until
  a consumer touches the value in JS.

**Where it surfaced.** Running the `neon-rising-feed` TypeScript path against a
local PostgreSQL 18 via `@sisal/pg`: `rising_score` came back as a string, so
the feed's `non-increasing` ordering check failed lexicographically and
`rising_score.toFixed(...)` would throw. The example itself uses
**`@sisal/neon`** (which returns a number), so it is _not_ affected and was left
uncoerced; but `@sisal/pg` (and `neon-hot-feed`'s `hot_score`, if run through
`@sisal/pg`) is.

**Proposed work.** Decode `float4`/`float8` to `number` in the `@sisal/pg`
result path so a `columns.doublePrecision` column reads back as the `number` it
infers, matching the other three adapters. `numeric`/`bigint` stay strings by
necessity (precision) and belong in the round-trip reference (item 5); this item
is specifically about the float types, where `number` is the correct, lossless
mapping.

**Affected.** `@sisal/pg` result decoding (`jsr:@db/postgres` type parsers) only
— `@sisal/neon` already returns a number;
[round-trip reference](#5-document-principled-divergences--round-trip-behavior-p2)
(item 5); the unified matrix (item 3) gains a tested "float round-trip" row.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- A `double precision` column read through `@sisal/pg` yields a JS `number`,
  matching `@sisal/neon` / `@sisal/sqlite` / `@sisal/libsql`.
- Integration coverage on pg asserts the runtime `typeof` is `number`.
- The float round-trip is added to the round-trip reference (item 5) and the
  unified matrix (item 3).

---

## 12. Data-modifying CTE builder (`WITH … INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE … RETURNING`) — P2

**Context.** Surfaced by
[`neon-rising-feed-ctes`](../examples/postgres-family-feed/README.md) (June
2026) — a deliberately function-free variant of the rising feed that models
every multi-step mutation as **one data-modifying CTE statement** (Neon
HTTP-friendly: one parameterized statement, no interactive transaction
callback). The activity recorder is a
`WITH input_data → validated_input →
bucket_data → actor_insert(INSERT…ON CONFLICT…RETURNING) → actor_flag →
bucket_upsert(INSERT…ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE…RETURNING)`
statement; the recompute paths are
`WITH score_windows → computed_score → updated_post(UPDATE…FROM…
RETURNING)`.

**Problem.** Sisal's CTE builder is **SELECT-only**: `db.$with(name).as(query)`
takes a `SetOperand` (a `SELECT`/compound) and `db.with(...ctes)` terminates in
`.select(...)`. There is no way to:

- use an `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE … RETURNING` as a CTE body,
- chain such stages (one CTE consuming another's `RETURNING`),
- or return rows from a `WITH`-wrapped data-modifying statement.

So the entire recorder and both recompute statements are hand-written raw `sql`
template strings — by far the largest raw-SQL surface in any example. The
builder's `db.batch` (independent statements) and interactive `transaction`
(multiple round trips / open session) do **not** cover the "several dependent
steps as one atomic statement" shape, which is exactly what data-modifying CTEs
give and what the serverless story wants.

**Where it surfaced.** `examples/neon-rising-feed-ctes/src/activity.ts`
(`recordPostActivity`) and `src/rising.ts` (`recomputePostRisingScore`,
`recomputeAllRisingScores`) — all raw `sql`.

**Proposed work (design).** A composable data-modifying CTE builder, e.g.
`db.$with("actor_insert").as(db.insert(actors).values(...).onConflictDoNothing().returning())`,
allowing CTE bodies to be insert/update/delete builders, downstream CTEs to
reference upstream `RETURNING` columns, and the chain to terminate in a final
`SELECT`/`RETURNING` whose row type is inferred. This is a large design (CTE
dependency typing, `EXCLUDED`/`ON CONFLICT` interplay, dialect support —
Postgres yes, SQLite supports data-modifying-CTE less fully) and overlaps with
item 8 (portable atomic operations).

**Affected.** `@sisal/orm` (`builders.ts` CTE types + `database.ts`
`$with`/`with` to accept mutation operands and a mutating terminal), each
adapter's render path.

**Acceptance criteria.**

- A `WITH` chain whose stages are `INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE … RETURNING` builders
  can be assembled and executed, returning a typed row.
- The `neon-rising-feed-ctes` recorder + recompute can drop to the builder (or
  the example documents precisely which sub-feature still needs raw SQL).
- Render tests pin the generated `WITH` SQL and parameter order.

**Status (🟢 done).** A CTE body can be an
`INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE … RETURNING` builder
(`db.$with("x").as(db.insert(t).values(...).returning())`, new `CteOperand`
type) and the `with(...)` chain terminates in a `SELECT` that reads its
`RETURNING`. **PostgreSQL-only** — the SQLite family rejects data-modifying
CTEs, so rendering one for a SQLite-family dialect throws a typed `OrmError`
(item-4 guard). Render tests pin the SQL + the guards; executed on PostgreSQL 18
and Neon, with a unified-matrix row (pg/neon ✅, sqlite/libsql ❌). The
**builder gaps are closed** (the three mutation builders were first refactored
to the `#state` + `#with(patch)` shape to make this safe):

1. **`UPDATE … FROM`** — `update(t).from(source)` (the rising-feed recompute's
   keystone). CTE columns referenced in `SET`/`WHERE` render as `"cte"."col"`.
2. **`DELETE … USING`** — `delete(t).using(source)`, **PostgreSQL-only** (a
   typed guard throws on the SQLite family).
3. **`INSERT … SELECT`** — `insert(t).select(query)`, so an insert reads another
   CTE's `RETURNING`
   (`with moved as (delete … returning) insert … select … from
   moved`).
4. **Mutating terminal** — `with(...).update/insert/delete(t)`.

Chained `SELECT` CTEs already worked. `UPDATE … FROM` / `INSERT … SELECT` run on
**every adapter** (modern SQLite/libSQL included); covered by
`packages/orm/mutation_cte_test.ts` and a `mutation joins` integration test on
all four engines. **Item 12c (the example proof) landed:** the
[`neon-rising-feed-ctes`](../examples/postgres-family-feed/README.md) recompute
(`recomputePostRisingScore` / `recomputeAllRisingScores`, `src/rising.ts`) is
now authored builder-native —
`db.with(scoreWindows, computedScore).update(posts).from(computedScore)
.returning(...)`
— and verified against **real Neon** to produce results identical to the prior
raw CTE and the TypeScript model. The recorder (`src/activity.ts`) stays a raw
CTE on purpose: its `onConflictDoUpdate` recompute of `activity_score` from
post-update counters, bridged by an `exists(...)` actor-flag across chained
data-modifying stages, is the one shape the builder still can't author — a
documented, honest pressure point rather than a blocker.

---

## 13. Typed raw-query result mapping — P3

**Context.** Also from
[`neon-rising-feed-ctes`](../examples/postgres-family-feed/README.md): because
the mutations are raw `sql` (item 12), their result rows come back through
`db.query<T>(sql\`…\`)`, which **trusts the caller's`<T>`generic** and does no
column-metadata-driven decoding. Each CTE's shape is hand-written
(`RecordedBucket`,`RecomputedPost`)
and trusted to match the SQL.

**Problem.** Raw-query rows are driver-shaped with no `defineTable`-driven
mapping: there is no way to say "decode this raw result as
`post_activity_buckets` rows" and get column naming, date/number coercion
(relates to item 11), and a checked row type. The hand-typed generic can
silently drift from the SQL, and on `@sisal/pg` the `double precision` columns
even come back as strings (item 11) with no typed layer to fix it.

**Proposed work.** A typed raw-result mapper — e.g.
`db.query(sql\`…\`).as(post_activity_buckets)`or`sql<typeof bucketRowShape>\`…\``— that applies a column descriptor map to a raw
result: physical→JS key naming, per-column decoding (float8→number, timestamptz→
Date/Temporal), and a derived row type. Pairs naturally with item 11 (float
decoding) and item 12 (typing a data-modifying CTE's`RETURNING`).

**Affected.** `@sisal/orm` (`database.ts` query surface + a result-metadata
mapper reusing the column descriptors `defineFunction`/select already carry).

**Acceptance criteria.**

- A raw query result can be decoded against a column-descriptor map, yielding a
  typed row with consistent naming and value coercion across adapters.
- The CTE example's hand-written `RecordedBucket` / `RecomputedPost` shapes can
  be derived from the table models instead of restated.

**Status (🟢 done).** Both input forms landed. A raw `db.query(...)` returns a
`MappableQueryResult` — still an awaitable `OrmQueryResult`, but with an
`.as(...)` method that decodes the raw driver rows (physical→JS column naming

- the same opt-in Temporal decoding the builder applies). Pass a `defineTable`
  model for typed `InferSelect<table>` rows, or a free-form `ColumnMap` for a
  result that doesn't match one table (a join/aggregate/CTE projection). Float
  coercion is already handled upstream (item 11, in the `@sisal/pg` executor).
  Unknown columns pass through; a plain `await db.query(...)` is unchanged. New
  `@sisal/orm` exports: `MappableQueryResult`, `ColumnMap`, `ColumnMapping` (the
  four adapter facades widen their `query` return to match). Verified by
  `packages/orm/raw_mapping_test.ts` and a `typed raw-query mapping` integration
  test on all four engines exercising both `.as(table)` and `.as(map)`; a
  unified-matrix row added. **The example proof landed:** in
  [`neon-rising-feed-ctes`](../examples/postgres-family-feed/README.md) the raw
  recorder now decodes its result with `db.query(...).as(postActivityBuckets)`,
  and both `RecordedBucket` (`InferSelect<typeof postActivityBuckets>`) and
  `RecomputedPost` (`Pick<Post, …>`) derive from the table models instead of
  being hand-restated — verified against real Neon.

---

## Tracking

When an item lands:

1. Implement it, add the integration tests on **every** supporting adapter, and
   move the paired [drizzle-parity](drizzle-parity.md) row where noted.
2. Update the unified feature matrix (item 3) and retire the corresponding rows
   from the per-engine docs.
3. Flip the item's status here to 🟢 and record it under `Unreleased` in
   [CHANGELOG.md](../CHANGELOG.md).

The roadmap is complete when the unified matrix shows, for every feature, either
a tested `✅` on every supporting adapter or a documented `❌`/`⚠️`/`—` with a
linked reason — and CI enforces that every `✅` is backed by a test.
```

### docs/v0.6.0-roadmap.md

URL: https://gilvandovieira.github.io/sisal/v0.6.0-roadmap.html

```md
---
title: v0.6.0 Roadmap
---

# Sisal v0.6.0 roadmap — Foundations & Readiness

Where [v0.5.0](v0.5.0-roadmap.md) makes the OLTP builder boringly correct across
the four dialects, v0.6.0 is the **readiness** milestone: it earns the right to
build later packages (and reach a fifth dialect) by proving the foundation can
carry them. It is deliberately **two parallel investigation workstreams plus one
readiness report**, all of which _prepare the ground and ship no new public
feature package_:

- **Workstream A — ETL Readiness Investigation.** The minimum ORM/core changes
  so a future `@sisal/etl` (v0.10) can be built without hacks.
- **Workstream C — MySQL Support Investigation.** Map what a `@sisal/mysql`
  adapter ([v0.7](v0.7.0-roadmap.md) builds it) needs, given that the renderer
  already carries a latent `"mysql"` dialect. Investigation only — no adapter.
- **npm-release readiness report (was "Workstream B").** Node.js + npm is **no
  longer a build workstream**. It sits on **no roadmap gate**, so it is
  investigated — not built — here: v0.6 produces the
  [npm-release readiness report](npm-release-readiness.md) (what must change to
  publish on npm) and ships **no npm package**. The build + dual publish is
  **deferred to [v0.13+](v0.13.0-roadmap.md), on demand**, enabled by the v0.8
  `@sisal/core` extraction and **blocked on a chosen npm name** (the `@sisal`
  scope is taken on npm). Publishing under an undecided name is the foot-gun the
  [sequencing audit](roadmap-sequencing-audit.md) warns about.

**This release builds nothing it investigates.** v0.6 answers "can Sisal express
ETL-shaped SQL cleanly?" and "what does MySQL support require?" — it ships
neither the ETL package nor the MySQL adapter, and no npm package. See
[release discipline](roadmap.md#release-discipline-non-negotiable).

## Finding (shipped in v0.5.1) — the `@sisal/pg` driver latency stall + postgres.js fix

Out-of-band during v0.6, benchmarking a real app on `@sisal/pg` surfaced a
severe per-query latency stall that is **entirely in the driver, not Sisal** —
and a validated fix. It shipped as [v0.5.1](../CHANGELOG.md#051---2026-06-30);
it is recorded here because it establishes the driver/adapter axis this
milestone builds on.

- **Symptom.** ~40 ms per _parameterized_ query on `@sisal/pg` — dead last in a
  feed-server benchmark (120 rps @ 90 ms p50 vs Drizzle's 5,550 @ 2 ms).
- **Root cause (source-verified).** `jsr:@db/postgres` never sets `TCP_NODELAY`
  and writes the extended-protocol Parse/Bind/Describe/Execute/Sync as five
  un-coalesced TCP segments → the classic Nagle × delayed-ACK deadlock (~40 ms
  timer). Sisal's builder (~0.02 ms) and executor (~0.01 ms) add nothing —
  isolated across five paths in [`perf/`](../perf/README.md).
- **Fix (validated, 40/40 × PostgreSQL 16/17/18).** A postgres.js-backed
  `PgPool` (`createPostgresJsPool`, or `connect({ driver: "postgres-js" })`);
  postgres.js sets `TCP_NODELAY` and pipelines. **No `@sisal/orm`/executor
  change.** The feed server went **120 → 6,774 rps (~56×), 90 → 1.6 ms**, moving
  Sisal from last to first (level with Drizzle, as both then ride postgres.js).
  Type parity with `@db/postgres` preserved (int8 → `BigInt`; naive
  `date`/`timestamp` via `new Date`). The default driver stays pure-JSR
  `@db/postgres`; `npm:postgres` is a lazily-imported opt-in, consistent with
  `@sisal/libsql`.
- **Implications carried forward.**
  - Driver identity now has a real axis (`db-postgres` | `postgres-js`), feeding
    the `(engine, version)` dialect-key / capability-descriptor thread the
    [sequencing audit](roadmap-sequencing-audit.md) tracks.
  - Open follow-ups: decide whether postgres.js becomes the **default** (a
    semver/deprecation call), and/or file the one-line `setNoDelay(true)` fix
    upstream in deno-postgres to rescue the pure-JSR path.
  - Guarded going forward by `deno task perf:pg` / `perf:pg:guard`; full report:
    [`perf/PG_ADAPTER_PERF_REPORT.md`](../perf/PG_ADAPTER_PERF_REPORT.md).

## Cross-adapter parity (v0.6.0)

The v0.5.1 driver raised a portability question: do the PostgreSQL-family
adapters return identical row shapes for the same query? Measured and pinned as
a gated test —
[`integration/cross_adapter_parity_test.ts`](../integration/cross_adapter_parity_test.ts)
(runs the same schema-free rich row through each adapter and diffs the decoded
values):

- **The two `@sisal/pg` drivers (`@db/postgres` ↔ postgres.js) decode
  byte-identically** across every scalar/array/temporal type — drop-in
  interchangeable, which is the correctness half of the 0.5.1 driver work.
- **`@sisal/neon` aligns with `@sisal/pg` on every type except `bigint`.** Neon
  (via `@neon/serverless`'s node-postgres type parsers) returns `bigint` as a
  **string**; both `@sisal/pg` drivers return **`BigInt`** (values are equal,
  only the JS type differs). `int`, `text`, `bool`, `numeric`, `float8`,
  `text[]`, `jsonb`, `time`, `timestamp`, `timestamptz`, and `null` all match. A
  raw `date` column is a `Date` at neon's _local_ vs pg's _UTC_ midnight — a
  process-TZ-dependent representation difference that resolves to the same
  `Temporal.PlainDate` under `temporal: { parse: true }`, so it is documented,
  not failed.

The test pins these facts the way [`docs/drizzle-parity.md`](drizzle-parity.md)
does: a driver behavior change (e.g. neon aligning `bigint`) fails it and forces
this doc to move in step.

**Open decision — align `bigint` across the pg family + neon.** Either neon →
`BigInt` (align to the pg drivers' current behavior), or all → `string` (matches
the "preserve precision" intent noted in `@sisal/pg`'s executor, and what neon
already does). Until decided, `bigint` is the one documented cross-adapter
portability caveat.

**Pain point (hit first-hand, 2026-07-01).** This divergence is not theoretical.
Making `postgres-family-activity-vectors` run over the whole family surfaced a
silent portability bug from exactly it: `getSimilarPosts` matched a source post
by its `bigint` `post_id` with a strict `s.post_id === postId` against a string
id. That quietly works on `@sisal/neon` (bigint → string) and throws
`no stats for post 2` on `@sisal/pg` (bigint → `BigInt`, so `2n === "2"` is
`false`). It type-checks, passes on the adapter it was written against, and
breaks on the sibling **with no warning** — the worst failure mode. Worked
around in the example by comparing `String(post_id)`, but that is a footgun
every consumer must remember, and concrete motivation to pick one representation
and align it. The
[cross-adapter parity test](../integration/cross_adapter_parity_test.ts) pins
the divergence so it can't regress silently once decided.

## Status legend

| Symbol | Meaning                                |
| ------ | -------------------------------------- |
| 🔴     | Not started                            |
| 🟡     | In progress / partial                  |
| 🟢     | Done — move the entry to the CHANGELOG |

## Priority summary — everything to track

All v0.6 tasks, consolidated. **Stream:** A = ETL readiness · C = MySQL
investigation. (The former Node.js/npm "Workstream B" is now the deferred
[npm-release readiness report](npm-release-readiness.md) and no longer tracked
as build tasks here.) Each ID has a detail section in the matching workstream
below. The June 2026 probe findings graduated on 2026-07-01, and **both
workstreams are now complete: A1-A6 and C1-C6 are all 🟢**. The ETL rollup is
composed + pinned per dialect and on real engines; the ETL runner correctness
substrate is documented as a design contract (`@sisal/etl`-owned checkpoint,
per-dialect run lock, atomic replay/backfill invariants) for v0.9 to make real
and tested; the `"mysql"` render path is pinned and now either renders correct
SQL or throws a typed guard — no construct renders wrong SQL; the driver,
type/DDL mapping, and MySQL-vs-MariaDB split are all decided and probe-verified,
consolidated in [`mysql-readiness.md`](mysql-readiness.md).

| ID | Task                                                                                  | Stream    | Priority | Effort | Status |
| -- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------- | -------- | ------ | ------ |
| A1 | Verify + pin the builder rollup (renders on PG — convert example + per-dialect tests) | A · ETL   | P1       | M      | 🟢     |
| A5 | Runnable PoC `postgres-family-activity-vectors` + future sibling contracts            | A · ETL   | P1       | M      | 🟢     |
| A6 | Document unbuilt analytics shapes (window fns, `ARRAY[]`/`unnest`) → v0.7/v0.8        | A · ETL   | P1       | S      | 🟢     |
| A2 | Advisory-lock / locking strategy per dialect                                          | A · ETL   | P2       | S      | 🟢     |
| A3 | Checkpoint / watermark table design                                                   | A · ETL   | P2       | S      | 🟢     |
| A4 | Idempotent load + backfill/replay design                                              | A · ETL   | P2       | S      | 🟢     |
| C1 | Pin the latent `"mysql"` render path as tests (render-ready)                          | C · MySQL | P1       | S      | 🟢     |
| C2 | Upsert → dialect-mapped `ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE` + `excluded()` (semantics recorded) | C · MySQL | P1       | M      | 🟢     |
| C3 | `RETURNING` → typed guard under `mysql` + dialect-guard sweep (no wrong SQL left)     | C · MySQL | P1       | M      | 🟢     |
| C6 | Driver survey + benchmarks → `mysql2` default, `mariadb` opt-in (perf/ report)        | C · MySQL | P1       | S      | 🟢     |
| C4 | Type/DDL mapping → probe-verified on MySQL 8.4 + MariaDB 11 (mysql-ddl-mapping.md)    | C · MySQL | P2       | M      | 🟢     |
| C5 | MySQL-vs-MariaDB split → one adapter + capability flags (mysql-readiness.md)          | C · MySQL | P2       | S      | 🟢     |

**P1 spine.** A1/A5/A6 (prove the ETL rollup + hand the analytics gaps to v0.7)
· C1/C2/C3/C6 (correct the `mysql` dialect so v0.7 can build `@sisal/mysql`).
Details, sequencing, and evidence per workstream below. The former Node "B
spine" (B1→B2→B4) now lives in the
[npm-release readiness report](npm-release-readiness.md).

---

# Workstream A — ETL Readiness Investigation

## Goal

Investigate and implement **only** the minimum ORM/core changes needed so a
future `@sisal/etl` package can express extract→transform→load as pushed-down
SQL without dropping to raw strings for the core shapes. Produce a **dialect
capability matrix** and a **proof-of-concept rollup** in docs/pseudo-code.

## Questions this release must answer

- Can Sisal express ETL-shaped SQL (grouped rollup → idempotent upsert) cleanly
  through the typed builder?
- Does the current internal query representation support this, or does it need a
  stronger SQL IR/AST? (See the [v0.8](v0.8.0-roadmap.md) IR work it feeds.)
- Can each dialect (pg/neon/sqlite/libsql) support the required SQL patterns?
- Which features belong in **core** and which belong in **dialect adapters**?
- What must be postponed to v0.8/v0.10?

## Scope — investigate & document support for

Set-based movement and aggregation (the rollup spine):

- `INSERT INTO … SELECT …` (insert-from-select) — **shipped in
  [v0.5 item 12](v0.5.0-roadmap.md)** (`insert(t).select(query)`, tested on all
  four engines). The original "single biggest ETL gap" is now a **verify + pin**
  task, not new work (see the PoC findings below).
- CTEs (`WITH`) — SELECT **and** data-modifying (Postgres-family) shipped in
  [v0.5 item 12](v0.5.0-roadmap.md); SQLite-family CTEs stay SELECT-only (typed
  guard).
- Aggregate expressions; `GROUP BY`; `HAVING` — **supported**.
- Conditional aggregation: `agg(…) FILTER (WHERE …)` — **shipped as `filter()`
  in [v0.5 item 9](v0.5.0-roadmap.md)**, and **native on the SQLite family**
  (the planned `CASE WHEN` fallback proved unnecessary).
- Upsert from select (`INSERT … SELECT … ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE`) — the pieces
  shipped (`insert().select()` + `onConflictDoUpdate`, v0.5); **verify they
  compose** into one statement per dialect.
- Typed raw-SQL escape hatches — **present** (`sql` tag binds params safely);
  the fallback for anything unbuilt.
- Computed expressions and expression **aliases** in projections — partial;
  needs a first-class typed alias surface for rollup metric columns.
- Date/time **bucket** abstraction — **shipped as `dateTrunc`/`dateBin` in
  [v0.5 item 9](v0.5.0-roadmap.md)** (per-dialect: `date_trunc`/`to_timestamp`
  on Postgres, `strftime`/`unixepoch` on the SQLite family).
- **Window functions** (`OVER`, moving averages, `lag`/`lead`, `rank`) —
  **absent, no builder at all**; the PoC's hard wall, owned by
  [v0.7](v0.7.0-roadmap.md).
- **Array projection** (`ARRAY[col, …]`) and **set-returning**
  (`unnest …
  WITH ORDINALITY`) — **absent**; raw SQL today (no array type on
  the SQLite family).

Execution & correctness primitives the runner will need:

- Batch execution; transaction boundaries — **today: supported** (`db.batch`,
  `db.transaction`). Note `db.batch` forbids cross-statement data dependencies.
- **Advisory locks or an equivalent locking strategy** — Postgres has
  `pg_advisory_lock` (already used internally by the migration history store — a
  reusable pattern); SQLite/libSQL need an equivalent (a lock row /
  `BEGIN
  IMMEDIATE`). **A2 documents the per-dialect strategy** in
  [08-job-queue-locking](../examples/advanced-sql-contracts/08-job-queue-locking.md);
  v0.9 must make it real and test-backed before v0.10 consumes it.
- **Checkpoint table design** — a watermark table (`job`, `window_end`,
  `updated_at`) so a run resumes from the last committed window. **A3 chooses an
  `@sisal/etl`-managed table** named `sisal_etl_checkpoints`; v0.9 may overturn
  that only with a recorded package-boundary reason.
- **Idempotent load strategy** — re-running a window must not double-count
  (upsert/replace semantics keyed by the rollup grain). **A4 fixes the
  invariant:** replace/upsert metrics, never additive increments.
- **Backfill/replay requirements** — re-run an arbitrary historical window range
  deterministically. **A4 defines `run`, `replay`, and `backfill` semantics** in
  [09-idempotent-backfill](../examples/advanced-sql-contracts/09-idempotent-backfill.md).

## Deliverable — dialect capability matrix

A matrix across **PostgreSQL · Neon · SQLite · libSQL** covering at least:
insert-from-select, upsert, returning, CTEs, window functions, filtered
aggregates, materialized views, advisory locks, date-bucket support, transaction
behavior, serverless/runtime caveats. Updated with **first-hand findings from
the `postgres-family-activity-vectors` PoC** (built June 2026 — see below);
several cells that were "no builder" in the original audit moved to "builder"
once v0.5 shipped:

| Capability                        | pg                                   | neon                                     | sqlite                            | libsql                                  |
| --------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| insert-from-select                | ✅ builder (v0.5 item 12)            | ✅ builder (v0.5 item 12)                | ✅ builder (v0.5 item 12)         | ✅ builder (v0.5 item 12)               |
| upsert (ON CONFLICT)              | ✅ builder                           | ✅ builder                               | ✅ builder                        | ✅ builder                              |
| upsert-from-select                | ✅ builder (A1: composed + pinned)   | ✅ (A1)                                  | ✅ (A1; WHERE/GROUP BY required)  | ✅ (A1; WHERE/GROUP BY required)        |
| returning                         | ✅                                   | ✅                                       | ✅                                | ✅                                      |
| filtered aggregates               | ✅ `filter()` (v0.5 item 9)          | ✅ `filter()` (v0.5)                     | ✅ `filter()` native (v0.5)       | ✅ `filter()` native (v0.5)             |
| date bucketing                    | ✅ `dateTrunc`/`dateBin` (v0.5)      | ✅ (v0.5)                                | ✅ (v0.5, `strftime`/`unixepoch`) | ✅ (v0.5)                               |
| CTEs (`WITH`)                     | ✅ builder                           | ✅ builder                               | ✅ builder (v0.5)                 | ✅ builder (v0.5)                       |
| data-modifying CTEs               | ✅ builder (v0.5 item 12)            | ✅ builder (v0.5 item 12)                | ❌ engine (typed guard)           | ❌ engine (typed guard)                 |
| **window functions**              | engine ✅ / **no builder → v0.7**    | engine ✅ / **no builder → v0.7**        | engine ✅ / **no builder → v0.7** | engine ✅ / **no builder → v0.7**       |
| **array construction `ARRAY[…]`** | engine ✅ / **no builder**           | engine ✅ / **no builder**               | ❌ no array type (`json_array`)   | ❌ no array type (`json_array`)         |
| **set-returning (`unnest`)**      | engine ✅ `unnest` / no builder      | engine ✅ / no builder                   | `json_each` / no builder          | `json_each` / no builder                |
| `CREATE FUNCTION`                 | engine ✅ / **no builder** (raw DDL) | engine ✅ / no builder                   | ❌ none                           | ❌ none                                 |
| materialized views                | engine ✅ / no DDL                   | engine ✅ / no DDL                       | ❌ none                           | ❌ none                                 |
| advisory locks                    | ✅ (pg_advisory_lock)                | ✅                                       | ❌ (use lock row)                 | ❌ (use lock row)                       |
| transactions                      | interactive ✅                       | interactive ✅ (WS Pool)                 | interactive ✅                    | interactive ✅                          |
| serverless caveats                | —                                    | WS Pool; single-statement; float8→number | embedded/FFI                      | Turso remote (hrana); ArrayBuffer BLOBs |

(Cells marked "no builder" work via the `sql` escape hatch today; the readiness
question is which deserve a typed builder in v0.8 vs stay raw. **Window
functions** are the one hard wall the PoC hit — owned by
[v0.7](v0.7.0-roadmap.md).)

## Deliverable — proof-of-concept rollup (now a runnable example)

A `post_events → post_hourly_stats` rollup demonstrating the desired shape (the
repo's existing `*-rising-feed` examples already bucket activity — this
generalizes that into an ETL rollup):

- **Extract** from a raw `post_events` table over a half-open time window
  `[from, until)`.
- **Group** by `post_id` and an hourly bucket.
- **Compute** `views`, `votes`, `comments`, `engagement_score`.
- **Load** into `post_hourly_stats` with idempotent upsert/replace.
- **Checkpoint** the window end (kept as a _future concern_ — design, don't
  ship).

The key SQL shape v0.6 must establish a path to express:

```sql
INSERT INTO post_hourly_stats (post_id, bucket, views, votes, comments, engagement_score)
SELECT post_id,
       date_trunc('hour', occurred_at) AS bucket,
       count(*) FILTER (WHERE kind = 'view')    AS views,
       count(*) FILTER (WHERE kind = 'vote')    AS votes,
       count(*) FILTER (WHERE kind = 'comment') AS comments,
       /* weighted */                            AS engagement_score
FROM post_events
WHERE occurred_at >= $from AND occurred_at < $until
GROUP BY post_id, date_trunc('hour', occurred_at)
ON CONFLICT (post_id, bucket)
DO UPDATE SET views = excluded.views, votes = excluded.votes,
              comments = excluded.comments, engagement_score = excluded.engagement_score;
```

This single statement exercises every audited gap: insert-from-select, a date
bucket, filtered aggregates (FILTER is native on the SQLite family since v0.5 —
no `CASE WHEN` fallback needed), and upsert-from-select. v0.6 decides which of
these become typed builders in v0.8.

## Findings — the `postgres-family-activity-vectors` PoC (built first-hand)

[`examples/postgres-family-activity-vectors`](../examples/postgres-family-activity-vectors/README.md)
is a **runnable** realization of this PoC on the PostgreSQL family (`@sisal/pg`
on `@db/postgres`, `@sisal/pg` on postgres.js, or `@sisal/neon`), verified
against real Postgres. It runs the full chain — raw `post_events` → hourly
buckets (a `fold` insert-from-select) → consolidated stats with **moving
averages** → an `ARRAY[…]` activity-vector projection → cosine similarity →
daily/monthly **rollups** + event **pruning**. What it found about how far the
v0.5 builder actually reaches (this is the substance of the readiness
investigation):

- **The rollup spine is largely builder-native already (post-v0.5).**
  `insert(t).select(query)` and `onConflictDoUpdate(...)` both ship (v0.5 item
  12, tested on all four engines), as do `filter(agg, cond)` and
  `dateTrunc`/`dateBin` (v0.5 item 9, native FILTER on the SQLite family). So
  `fold_events_to_buckets` / `rollup_daily` / `rollup_monthly`
  (`INSERT … SELECT … GROUP BY … FILTER … ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE`) are **probably
  expressible through the builder today**. The v0.6 question narrows from "can
  we express the rollup?" to **"verify the full statement composes + renders per
  dialect, and pin it"** — the example currently uses raw SQL functions for
  framing, so converting them is the concrete verification task (and the honest
  way to find the limit instead of hiding it).
- **Window functions are the one hard wall — no builder of any kind.** The
  moving averages
  (`avg(...) OVER (PARTITION BY … ORDER BY … ROWS BETWEEN 5
  PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)`)
  cannot be expressed at all: no `OVER`, `PARTITION BY`, `ROWS`/`RANGE`,
  `lag`/`lead`, `rank`, `row_number`. This is the single biggest analytics gap
  and is **owned by [v0.7](v0.7.0-roadmap.md)**; v0.6 records it as the blocking
  discovery.
- **Array construction and set-returning have no builder.** Storing a JS array
  into an array column is builder-native, but **projecting named columns into a
  SQL `ARRAY[…]`** (the feature-vector projection) and consuming one with
  `unnest … WITH ORDINALITY` are raw. On the SQLite family there is **no array
  type at all** (`json_array`/`json_each` instead). That sibling remains a
  future contract, not a v0.6 runnable example, because the shape is genuinely
  different rather than a small adapter switch.
- **`CREATE FUNCTION` has no builder.** The whole `app.*` computation engine is
  raw `.sql`. (v0.5 B7 can _carry_ such DDL in a snapshot; it does not _emit_ a
  typed function.)
- **Clean / no gap:** the batch event insert, the stats/posts reads, and
  similarity candidate loading are all clean builder `select`; bigint ids
  round-trip as strings (documented); and the typed function caller
  (`defineFunction`/`db.call`) exists and could replace the raw `sql` calls.

**Net for v0.6:** the ETL rollup spine is closer to done than the original audit
assumed (v0.5 delivered the pieces) — so a primary v0.6 deliverable is to
**prove the builder expresses the rollup end-to-end and pin per-dialect render
tests**, rather than design new primitives. The genuinely-unbuilt analytics
shapes the PoC surfaced (window functions, array/`unnest` projection) are
recorded here and handed to [v0.7](v0.7.0-roadmap.md)/[v0.8](v0.8.0-roadmap.md).

## Task detail (Workstream A)

(Priority/effort/status are in the top tracker; this is the per-task body.)

- **A1 — Verify + pin the builder rollup. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).** The full
  statement — `insert().select()` + `filter` + `dateTrunc` + `groupBy` +
  `onConflictDoUpdate` — composes and is pinned three ways:
  - **Render tests** (`packages/orm/etl_rollup_test.ts`): the canonical
    `post_events → post_hourly_stats` statement, exact text + cross-clause
    parameter order, for the `postgres` and `sqlite` dialects.
  - **Real-engine tests**: an `ETL rollup` integration test in each of the four
    feature suites (fold → idempotent re-run → late-event upsert), a new all-✅
    row in [the feature matrix](feature-matrix.md). Verified on pg16/17/18, Neon
    (wsproxy), SQLite, and libSQL.
  - **Example conversion**: the activity-vectors example's
    `fold_events_to_buckets` / `rollup_daily` / `rollup_monthly` /
    `prune_events` SQL functions are now builder statements (`src/events.ts`,
    `src/retention.ts`); only the window-function stats and array/`unnest`
    similarity remain raw (the A6 walls).
  - **Findings.** (1) The residual raw seam inside the composed statement is
    `coalesce(...)` (via the `sql` tag); proposed-row references use Sisal's
    exported `excluded()` helper. A typed `coalesce` surface is a v0.8
    candidate, not a blocker. (2) **SQLite-family parse quirk (pinned):** a
    _bare_ upsert-from-select (`INSERT … SELECT … FROM t ON CONFLICT …` with
    nothing after the FROM) is rejected — the engine can't tell the upsert's
    `ON` from a join constraint. Any window `WHERE` or rollup `GROUP BY`
    disambiguates, so real ETL shapes always parse; pinned in the sqlite/libsql
    suites.
- **A5 — Runnable PoC + siblings. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).**
  [`postgres-family-activity-vectors`](../examples/postgres-family-activity-vectors/README.md)
  is the v0.6 runnable PoC and now covers the PostgreSQL family: `@sisal/pg` on
  `@db/postgres`, `@sisal/pg` on postgres.js, and `@sisal/neon` via
  `SISAL_ADAPTER`. No separate `postgres` sibling is needed after the
  dialect-family example consolidation. The SQLite/libSQL sibling is
  deliberately deferred: the Postgres example depends on stored functions,
  `ARRAY[…]`, and `unnest WITH ORDINALITY`, while the SQLite family has no
  stored functions or array type. Those shapes are preserved as
  documentation-only future contracts, not a v0.6 runnable package.
- **A6 — Hand off the analytics gaps. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).** The
  genuinely-unbuilt shapes the PoC hit — window functions, `ARRAY[…]`
  projection, `unnest WITH ORDINALITY`, plus the SQLite-family `json_array` /
  `json_each` alternative — are written up as future contracts in
  [`examples/advanced-sql-contracts/`](../examples/advanced-sql-contracts/README.md)
  and routed to [v0.7](v0.7.0-roadmap.md)/[v0.8](v0.8.0-roadmap.md). The direct
  handoff docs are
  [02-window-analytics](../examples/advanced-sql-contracts/02-window-analytics.md),
  [03-sessionization](../examples/advanced-sql-contracts/03-sessionization.md),
  [04-top-n-per-group](../examples/advanced-sql-contracts/04-top-n-per-group.md),
  and
  [10-json-table-extraction](../examples/advanced-sql-contracts/10-json-table-extraction.md).
- **A2 — Locking strategy. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).** The v0.6 decision is a coarse
  ETL run lock keyed by job, documented in
  [08-job-queue-locking](../examples/advanced-sql-contracts/08-job-queue-locking.md).
  PostgreSQL/Neon use a session-scoped `pg_try_advisory_lock` /
  `pg_advisory_unlock` on a stable `sisal:etl:<job>` key hashed to 64-bit (the
  same class of pattern already used by the migration history store).
  SQLite/libSQL use `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` around lock/load/checkpoint for supported
  local or interactive runs, and otherwise capability-gate rather than silently
  degrade. Future MySQL/MariaDB use `GET_LOCK(name, 0)` / `RELEASE_LOCK(name)`.
  v0.9 owns the implementation and per-engine tests before v0.10 consumes it.
- **A3 — Checkpoint design. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).** The v0.6 default is an
  `@sisal/etl`-managed checkpoint table named `sisal_etl_checkpoints` with
  logical columns `job` (primary key), `window_end`, and `updated_at`;
  adapter-specific physical timestamp types are allowed. This keeps the future
  `etl` package off `@sisal/migrate` unless v0.9 reverses the decision with a
  recorded package-boundary reason.
- **A4 — Idempotent load + backfill/replay. ✅ Done (2026-07-01).** A run
  processes half-open windows `[from, until)`, writes through upsert/replace
  semantics keyed by the rollup grain, and advances the checkpoint atomically
  with the load. `run()` acquires the job lock, reads the checkpoint, loads one
  next window, and advances `window_end`; `replay(window)` re-runs one explicit
  window without advancing the checkpoint by default; `backfill(range)` iterates
  explicit windows and does not disturb the live checkpoint by default.
  Idempotency forbids additive metric increments in the load path.
  **Replay-vs-retention invariant (added 2026-07-01):** replace-semantics replay
  over a window whose raw source rows were already pruned would silently
  overwrite good rollups with zeros — so the checkpoint contract carries a
  per-job `pruned_before` replay horizon, advanced atomically with the prune
  (never lagging the delete), and `replay`/`backfill` refuse windows behind it
  with a typed error (explicit unsafe override, mirroring
  `.unsafeAllowAllRows()`). Recorded in
  [09-idempotent-backfill](../examples/advanced-sql-contracts/09-idempotent-backfill.md);
  v0.9 implements and tests it with the rest of the substrate.

## Workstream A non-goals

No public `@sisal/etl` package; no scheduler; no worker daemon; no Rust/native
engine; no DuckDB adapter; no dashboard API; no full analytics module.

---

# npm-release readiness (formerly "Workstream B")

Node.js + npm is **no longer a build workstream in v0.6**. It is on **no roadmap
gate** — nothing in v0.7–v0.12 (MySQL, `@sisal/core`, ETL, analytics) depends on
npm; the feature line runs entirely on Deno/JSR. So v0.6 **investigates** it and
ships **no npm package**.

**The deliverable is a document:** the
[npm-release readiness report](npm-release-readiness.md). It records, from the
June 2026 Node feasibility audit, exactly what must change to publish on npm —
package manifests + `exports`, the six `jsr:`/`npm:` import sites, the
`.ts`→`.js` build (`dnt`), the dual-runtime test harness, and CI/publish — plus
the **one blocker that makes this a decide-first item: the npm name.** The
`@sisal` scope is taken on npm, and publishing under an undecided placeholder
name is a one-way door — the exact premature-public-commitment foot-gun the
[sequencing audit](roadmap-sequencing-audit.md) flags.

**Deferral.** The build + dual publish is **deferred to
[v0.13+](v0.13.0-roadmap.md), on demand**:

- **Trigger:** real Node-user demand **and** a chosen npm name.
- **Enabler:** the v0.8 `@sisal/core` extraction (the one clean `.ts`→`.js`
  build boundary).
- **Keep-the-door-open (cheap, do now):** pick a MySQL driver that works on
  **Deno _and_ Node** (see C6), and don't regress the runtime-agnostic patterns
  the audit found (drivers injectable, `Deno` read via `globalThis`).

## v0.6 npm deliverable

The committed [npm-release readiness report](npm-release-readiness.md) — no
manifests, no build, no npm publish in v0.6.

---

# Workstream C — MySQL Support Investigation

## Goal

Determine exactly what a future `@sisal/mysql` adapter requires, so
[v0.7](v0.7.0-roadmap.md) can implement it without surprises — and decide which
work is **core** (shared dialect plumbing) vs **adapter** (driver, executor,
DDL, type mapping). Investigation and a capability report only; **no adapter
ships in v0.6.**

## What grounds this — MySQL is latent, not absent

A June 2026 audit found the renderer already half-knows MySQL, but no adapter
exists:

- **Already present (core render layer):** `SqlDialect` includes `"mysql"`
  (`core/sql.ts`); the serializable snapshot dialect type includes `"mysql"`
  (`schema.ts`); `quoteIdentifier` emits **backtick** quoting for `mysql`
  (`core/sql.ts`); placeholders fall through to `?` (only `postgres` uses `$N`);
  `ilike` degrades to case-insensitive `LIKE` (MySQL has no `ILIKE`); and the
  builder comments already treat `FOR UPDATE`/`FOR SHARE` and row-value tuples
  as Postgres/**MySQL**-supported.
- **Absent (everything an adapter needs):** there is **no `@sisal/mysql`
  package** — no `MYSQL_DIALECT` constant, no executor, no driver/pool, no
  migrate wiring, **no MySQL DDL generator** (only
  `generate{Postgres,Sqlite,Libsql}UpStatements` exist), no column-type → MySQL
  type mapping, no integration suite, no `docs/mysql-compatibility.md`, no
  feature-matrix column, and no chosen MySQL driver.

## Scope — investigate & document

- **Driver choice & runtime** — survey MySQL drivers usable from Deno (and Node,
  per the [npm-release readiness report](npm-release-readiness.md) — pick one
  that works on both so npm stays cheap later): `jsr`/`npm` options,
  connection/pool API, prepared-statement and parameter-binding model, TLS, and
  any serverless variants (e.g. PlanetScale-style HTTP). Note the
  injectable-executor seam every adapter uses.
- **Dialect completeness** — verify/extend the latent `"mysql"` render path:
  identifier quoting (backtick + escaping), `?` placeholders, `LIMIT/OFFSET`,
  `ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE` vs `ON CONFLICT` (MySQL upsert differs
  **materially** from Postgres/SQLite), `RETURNING` (MySQL lacks it pre-8.0.21 /
  MariaDB differences), `ILIKE`→`LIKE`, `FOR UPDATE`/`FOR SHARE`, and whether
  the `dialectGuard` set is correct for MySQL (row locking is supported;
  `distinctOn` and array operators are not — **corrected by C3's guard sweep**).
- **Type system & round-trips** — MySQL type mapping (`BOOLEAN`=`TINYINT(1)`,
  `JSON` type, `DATETIME`/`TIMESTAMP`/timezone handling, `DECIMAL`/`BIGINT`
  string-vs-number like the pg `numeric` case), and `bytea`/`BLOB` shapes.
  **Answered by C4** — probe-verified table in
  [`mysql-ddl-mapping.md`](mysql-ddl-mapping.md).
- **DDL generation** — what a `generateMysqlUpStatements` must emit (engine,
  charset/collation, `AUTO_INCREMENT`, index/`CHECK` support quirks). **Answered
  by C4** — generation rules + version floor in
  [`mysql-ddl-mapping.md`](mysql-ddl-mapping.md).
- **MySQL vs MariaDB** — decide whether "MySQL" means MySQL 8+, MariaDB, or
  both, and where they diverge (`RETURNING`, sequences, JSON functions).
  **Answered by C5** — both, one adapter, MySQL-8-first; probe-verified
  divergence matrix in [`mysql-readiness.md`](mysql-readiness.md).
- **Upsert semantics** — was the biggest builder gap: MySQL's
  `INSERT … ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE` (with `VALUES()`/`new.col`) does not map
  1:1 onto the existing `onConflict*` builder. **Abstraction decided +
  implemented in C2** (dialect-mapped `onConflict*` + `excluded()`; see the task
  detail).

## Deliverable

A **MySQL-readiness report** + a **draft capability matrix row** (a fifth column
alongside pg/neon/sqlite/libsql) covering upsert, returning, CTEs, window
functions, filtered aggregates, row locking, date bucketing, and transaction
behavior — classifying each as **render-ready today / needs core work / needs
adapter work / MySQL-specific divergence**.

## Task detail (Workstream C) — the v0.7 `@sisal/mysql` enabler

(Priority/effort/status are in the top tracker; this is the per-task body.)
Status reflects a first-hand probe (June 2026) that rendered real builder
queries under the `"mysql"` dialect — so the **render-ready** vs **gap** calls
are verified, not assumed.

- **C1 — Pin the `"mysql"` render path. ✅ Done (2026-07-01)** —
  `packages/orm/mysql_dialect_test.ts`. **Pinned render-ready:** backtick
  quoting (`` `posts`.`id` ``), `?` placeholders, `ilike`→`like`, plain
  `SELECT`/`INSERT`, `FOR UPDATE` row locking,
  `onConflictDoUpdate`/`onConflictDoNothing` rendering
  `ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE`, and `excluded()` rendering MySQL's portable
  `values(col)` proposed-row reference. **Pinned caveats**, drizzle-parity style
  — fixing any fails the pin and must move this doc: MySQL upsert conflict
  targets validate but do not render, conflict `where` throws
  `ORM_DIALECT_UNSUPPORTED`, the C3 `returning *` gap (since resolved — C3
  guards it), **`distinctOn` unguarded** (a new finding the probe missed; since
  corrected by C3's guard sweep), and the portable date helpers
  (`dateTrunc`/`now`/`dateBin`) throwing `ORM_DIALECT_UNSUPPORTED` (only
  `postgres`+`sqlite` variants exist — the one remaining pinned gap, v0.7
  adapter work).
- **C2 — Upsert divergence. ✅ Done (2026-07-01) — dialect-mapped, semantics
  recorded.** The decision (answering the open question below): **one
  dialect-mapped `onConflict*` surface, not a separate Drizzle-style
  `onDuplicateKeyUpdate`** — the ETL load step (v0.10) needs the same builder
  statement to render on every dialect, and forking the callsite per dialect is
  the leak the risk section warns about. Under `mysql`,
  `onConflictDoUpdate`/`onConflictDoNothing` render `ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE` in
  the core render path, pinned by `packages/orm/mysql_dialect_test.ts`, with
  these recorded semantics:
  - **Conflict target: validated, not rendered.** ODKU fires on ANY unique-key
    violation; the API still requires and validates the target, and the
    semantics coincide with `ON CONFLICT` exactly when the target is the table's
    only unique constraint — the normal upsert grain. This is the one documented
    semantic widening.
  - **Conflict `where`: no MySQL equivalent.** Rendering one under `mysql`
    throws a typed `ORM_DIALECT_UNSUPPORTED` (pinned).
  - **`excluded(column)` → `values(col)`** — the one proposed-row spelling valid
    across MySQL 5.7→9.x (deprecated since 8.0.20 but present through the
    current manuals) **and** MariaDB, which never adopted the alias form.
    MySQL's 8.0.19+ `AS new` row alias is MySQL-only, so switching to it is
    version/variant-aware work owned by the `(engine, version)` dialect-key
    thread — not the version-less v0.6 dialect. The typed helper also fixes a
    latent naming-strategy footgun: a raw `` sql`excluded.hotScore` `` misses
    the `hot_score` physical mapping; `excluded()` resolves and quotes it
    (pinned in `set_values_expr_test.ts`).
  - **`onConflictDoNothing` → a no-op self-assignment** (`col = col`; the first
    resolvable target column, else the first primary-key column, else the first
    declared column). `INSERT IGNORE` was rejected — it swallows unrelated
    errors (data truncation, FK violations), which is not "do nothing on
    conflict".

  v0.7's adapter consumes this as-is; the row-alias upgrade and the MariaDB
  variant split are C5 / dialect-identity inputs.
- **C3 — `RETURNING` divergence. ✅ Done (2026-07-01) — typed guard +
  dialect-guard sweep.** The decision: under the version-less `"mysql"` dialect,
  **any `returning()` on insert/update/delete throws a typed
  `ORM_DIALECT_UNSUPPORTED` at render time** instead of emitting SQL the engine
  rejects. Why a guard and not MariaDB emission: MySQL 8/9 has **no**
  `RETURNING` at all, and MariaDB's support is per-statement **and** per-version
  (`DELETE` 10.0.5+, `INSERT`/`REPLACE` 10.5+, `UPDATE` only 13.0+ and
  single-table) — so even the MariaDB variant needs the `(engine, version)`
  dialect key; it cannot be expressed on a version-less dialect. The
  fetch-by-key fallback is an adapter/executor concern (a second round trip, not
  a render rewrite) — a v0.7 `@sisal/mysql` design input. Postgres/SQLite
  rendering is unchanged (both support `RETURNING`; the guard's misleading
  hardcoded "PostgreSQL-only" error suffix was dropped). **Bundled dialect-guard
  sweep** — the same change corrected every guard C1 pinned as wrong for MySQL,
  so the dialect now throws rather than rendering invalid SQL: `distinctOn`
  (+`mysql`), array operators (+`mysql`), data-modifying CTEs (+`mysql` —
  MySQL/MariaDB `WITH` is `SELECT`-only), `DELETE … USING` (+`mysql` — MySQL's
  multi-table form needs the target repeated in `USING`; mapping it is v0.7
  work), and a **new** guard on `UPDATE … FROM` (MySQL's equivalent is the
  multi-table `UPDATE t, s SET …` shape). All pinned in
  `packages/orm/mysql_dialect_test.ts`; row locking (`FOR UPDATE`/`FOR SHARE`)
  correctly stays unguarded.
- **C6 — Driver survey (Deno + Node). ✅ Done (2026-07-01) — benchmarked, driver
  chosen.** Full report with numbers:
  [`perf/MYSQL_DRIVER_SURVEY.md`](../perf/MYSQL_DRIVER_SURVEY.md) (probe:
  `perf/mysql_driver_survey.ts`, `deno task perf:mysql`; run against real MySQL
  8.4.10 **and** MariaDB 11.8.8 from Deno 2.9, dual-runtime-verified on Node
  26). **Chosen default: `npm:mysql2`** (MIT, de-facto standard, Deno+Node
  verified, prepared statements + pool + TLS, no `@db/postgres`-class stall —
  parameterized p50 ≈ 0.08 ms) **with `supportBigNumbers` + `bigNumberStrings`
  mandatory in the executor** — mysql2's default `BIGINT` decode is silently
  lossy past 2⁵³ (measured: …93 → …92); with the options it decodes to a
  precision-safe string, matching `@sisal/neon`'s convention. **Opt-in
  candidate: `npm:mariadb`** — fastest on every path (~1.5–2× lower sequential
  p50, ~24.5k vs ~20k pooled qps), `BigInt`-correct by default, but LGPL-2.1 →
  lazily-imported opt-in via the injectable-executor seam (the postgres.js
  pattern). **Watch: `jsr:@db/mysql`** — works (MySQL 8 `caching_sha2` auth,
  `BigInt`-correct, competitive latency) but rc-only (`3.0.0-rc.1`,
  `latest: null`) and Deno-only, breaking the npm door-open constraint.
  **Serverless: `npm:@planetscale/database`** = the future `@sisal/neon`-style
  adapter variant (HTTP, service-bound; survey-only). Key negative result: **no
  candidate has the Nagle-class parameterized-query stall** the pg survey found.
- **C4 — Type/DDL mapping. ✅ Done (2026-07-01) — probe-verified on both
  engines.** Full report: [`mysql-ddl-mapping.md`](mysql-ddl-mapping.md); probe:
  `perf/mysql_ddl_probe.ts` (`deno task perf:mysql:ddl`), run against MySQL
  8.4.10 **and** MariaDB 11.8.8 with the C6-chosen driver. Every column kind is
  mapped (probe-applied + round-tripped): `boolean` → `BOOLEAN` (= `TINYINT(1)`,
  `0`/`1` round-trip like the SQLite family), serial/bigserial →
  `INT`/`BIGINT AUTO_INCREMENT` (placement validated at generation time — must
  be a key, one per table), `json`/`jsonb`/`.array()` → `JSON` (parsed on MySQL,
  **text on MariaDB** — its `JSON` is a `LONGTEXT` alias), `timestamp` →
  `DATETIME(6)`, `timestamptz` → `TIMESTAMP(6) NULL` (2038 cliff on MySQL,
  probe-confirmed; MariaDB extended), `numeric`/`bigint` → string round-trips,
  `bytea` → `LONGBLOB`, `uuid` → `CHAR(36)` (+ `DEFAULT (uuid())` works on
  both). DDL rules with probe findings behind each: **table-level FKs only**
  (inline `REFERENCES` is _silently ignored_ by MySQL — the pg generator's
  after-CREATE ordering transfers as-is), paren-wrapped expression defaults (the
  only form both engines accept on TEXT/JSON columns), no
  `CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS` (MySQL rejects), DESC ✓ / functional MySQL-8-only
  / partial unsupported → typed generation errors, `CHECK` enforced on both,
  **no `ENGINE`/`CHARSET` clause** (the collation-name trap), `GET_LOCK` as the
  migrator's advisory-lock analogue. Version floor: MySQL ≥ 8.0.16, MariaDB ≥
  10.10. The MariaDB divergence table feeds C5 directly.
- **C5 — MySQL-vs-MariaDB capability split. ✅ Done (2026-07-01) — decided +
  probe-verified.** Full consolidated report:
  [`mysql-readiness.md`](mysql-readiness.md) (this is also the workstream's
  MySQL-readiness deliverable); probe: `perf/mysql_variant_probe.ts`
  (`deno task perf:mysql:variant`, run on MySQL 8.4.10 + MariaDB 11.8.8).
  **Decision: one `@sisal/mysql` adapter, MySQL ≥ 8.0.16 baseline, MariaDB ≥
  10.10 on the same adapter via a variant-aware capability descriptor** — not a
  second package. The ~20-row divergence matrix was executed live (the C2/C3
  research claims graduated to executed facts: `INSERT`/`DELETE …
  RETURNING`
  MariaDB-only, `UPDATE … RETURNING` neither, ODKU `VALUES()` both / row alias
  MySQL-only), plus new findings: `LATERAL` and the JSON `->>` operator are
  MySQL-only, `CREATE SEQUENCE` and the native `UUID` type are MariaDB-only.
  **Two last wrong-SQL renders found and fixed:** `fullJoin` (no
  `FULL OUTER JOIN` on either engine) and `filter()` (no `FILTER` clause on
  either engine) now throw typed guards — the `CASE WHEN` fallback for
  `filter()` is v0.7 core work. The report also records the **dialect-identity
  decision** (see Open questions) and the draft fifth capability-matrix column.

**Handoff to v0.7 — workstream C is complete.** The `mysql` dialect renders
correct SQL (upsert, quoting, placeholders, locking) or throws a typed guard
(`RETURNING`, `UPDATE … FROM`, `DELETE … USING`, data-modifying CTEs,
`distinctOn`, array operators, `FULL JOIN`, `filter()`, the date helpers) — **no
known construct renders wrong SQL**. The build list, core-vs-adapter classified,
lives in [`mysql-readiness.md`](mysql-readiness.md): the
`(engine, variant, version)` axis + guard-to-capability-predicate
generalization, the date-helper/`filter()` mysql renderings (core), and the
driver/executor/DDL-generator/migrator/scenario work (adapter).

## Workstream C non-goals

No `@sisal/mysql` package; no driver dependency added; no MySQL integration
tests; no DDL generator implementation. Those are [v0.7](v0.7.0-roadmap.md).

---

# Shared sections

## Risks

- **A stronger IR may be required** — if insert-from-select / filtered
  aggregates can't be expressed cleanly as fragments, v0.8 IR work grows. v0.6's
  job is to surface this early, not absorb it.
- **Dialect divergence on data-modifying CTEs** — Postgres supports them; SQLite
  "less fully". Risk of a builder that only works on one family.
- **Scope creep into ETL** — the temptation to "just build the runner." Held off
  by the explicit non-goals.
- **Locking portability** — `pg_advisory_lock` has no SQLite equivalent; a wrong
  abstraction here leaks into v0.10.
- **Version/variant-blind dialect identity** — `SqlDialect`/`SisalDialectName`
  are version-less 4-value unions (they even collapse neon→postgres,
  libsql→sqlite); MySQL-vs-MariaDB and 5.7-vs-8 need a finer key. Decide the
  `(engine, version)` shape **now**, before v0.7 ships `@sisal/mysql` and v0.8
  freezes the IR — a late axis breaks the exported `dialectGuard`/IR surface
  (the snapshot is versioned via `SCHEMA_SNAPSHOT_VERSION` and can migrate; the
  guard signature can't). See the
  [sequencing audit](roadmap-sequencing-audit.md).
- **(npm) deferred, not dropped** — the build + dual publish moves to v0.13+;
  the only risk is forgetting the cheap door-open constraints (Node-capable
  driver, runtime-agnostic code), captured in the
  [npm-release readiness report](npm-release-readiness.md).
- **(C) MySQL upsert is not Postgres upsert** — `ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE`
  differs materially from `ON CONFLICT`; a forced-fit abstraction would leak
  into v0.7 and the ETL load step. **Retired by C2 + C3 (2026-07-01):** the
  `"mysql"` render path is pinned by tests
  (`packages/orm/mysql_dialect_test.ts`) and no known construct renders wrong
  SQL — the upsert is dialect-mapped and everything MySQL can't express
  (`RETURNING`, `UPDATE … FROM`, `DELETE … USING`, data-modifying CTEs,
  `distinctOn`, array operators, the date helpers) throws a typed
  `ORM_DIALECT_UNSUPPORTED` at render time.

## Open questions

- ~~Should `INSERT … SELECT` be a first-class builder, or only reachable through
  a data-modifying CTE (v0.5 item 12)?~~ **Answered by v0.5/A1:** it is
  first-class as `insert(t).select(query)` and the ETL rollup pins the composed
  statement on all four current adapters.
- Does the readiness work need an introspectable expression AST, or is the
  compose-only fragment IR enough to compile ETL SQL? (Feeds v0.8.)
- ~~Checkpoint table: owned by `@sisal/etl`, or a `@sisal/migrate`-managed
  system table?~~ **Answered by A3:** default to an `@sisal/etl`-managed
  `sisal_etl_checkpoints` table; v0.9 may reverse only with a recorded
  package-boundary reason and an architecture update.
- ~~Is a portable advisory-lock abstraction worth it, or is locking
  Postgres-first with documented degradation on SQLite/libSQL?~~ **Answered by
  A2:** yes, but as a capability-gated coarse ETL run lock:
  `pg_try_advisory_lock` / `pg_advisory_unlock` on PostgreSQL/Neon,
  `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` on supported SQLite/libSQL runs, and future MySQL/MariaDB
  `GET_LOCK` / `RELEASE_LOCK`. v0.9 must implement and test this before v0.10
  consumes it.
- **(C)** ~~Does "MySQL" mean MySQL 8+, MariaDB, or both?~~ **Answered by C5
  (2026-07-01): both, one adapter** — MySQL ≥ 8.0.16 baseline, MariaDB ≥ 10.10
  on the same adapter via a variant-aware capability descriptor; the
  probe-verified divergence matrix and rationale live in
  [`mysql-readiness.md`](mysql-readiness.md). ~~How is `RETURNING`'s uneven
  support handled?~~ **Answered by C3 (2026-07-01): a typed render-time guard on
  the version-less dialect** — MariaDB emission is per-statement and per-version
  (`DELETE` 10.0.5+, `INSERT` 10.5+, `UPDATE` 13.0+), so it waits for the
  `(engine, version)` key; the fetch-by-key fallback is a v0.7 adapter/executor
  design input. ~~Should upsert get a MySQL-aware `onDuplicateKeyUpdate`
  surface, or a dialect-mapped `onConflict`?~~ **Answered by C2 (2026-07-01):
  dialect-mapped `onConflict*` + the typed `excluded()` helper** — see the C2
  task detail for the recorded target/`where`/`VALUES()` semantics.
- ~~**Dialect identity (decide, don't defer):** is the dialect key
  `(engine, version)` with MySQL/MariaDB as distinct variants, and is
  `dialectGuard` generalized to a version-aware capability predicate?~~
  **Decided (C5, 2026-07-01): yes** — recorded in
  [`mysql-readiness.md`](mysql-readiness.md) (decision 2): the snapshot dialect
  gains an optional variant/version axis (the snapshot is versioned and can
  migrate), `SqlDialect` stays the render key, and `dialectGuard` generalizes to
  a version-aware capability predicate, implemented in **v0.7 alongside the
  adapter and before the v0.8 IR freeze**. C5's live probes are the empirical
  forcing function (the same `"mysql"` SQL splits both ways across the
  variants).

## Acceptance criteria

- A written **ETL-readiness report** answering the five questions, with the
  capability matrix and the `post_events → post_hourly_stats` PoC, committed to
  docs.
- Each required SQL shape is classified: **buildable today / needs a v0.8
  builder / stays raw**, per dialect.
- Any **minimum** core changes identified as truly prerequisite (not the ETL
  package itself) are itemized with effort, or explicitly deferred to v0.8.
- **(npm)** a committed [npm-release readiness report](npm-release-readiness.md)
  (what must change to publish, plus the name blocker); **no** npm package,
  build, or publish in v0.6.
- **(dialect identity)** the `(engine, version)` dialect-key decision is
  recorded (variant/version field on the snapshot dialect; `dialectGuard` →
  version-aware capability predicate), so v0.7/v0.8 build on it instead of
  around it.
- **(C)** A committed **MySQL-readiness report**, a draft capability-matrix
  column, and a chosen driver, with every required item classified
  core-vs-adapter and the upsert/`RETURNING`/type-mapping divergences documented
  for v0.7.
- **(A2-A4)** The ETL-runner correctness substrate is documented but not built:
  a per-dialect run-lock strategy, the `@sisal/etl`-managed
  `sisal_etl_checkpoints` contract (including the `pruned_before` replay
  horizon), and atomic idempotent `run`/`replay`/`backfill` semantics — with the
  replay-vs-retention refusal — are recorded for v0.9 to make real and
  test-backed.
- No public ETL package, scheduler, runtime, or MySQL adapter exists in the
  tree.

## Affected packages
